pattern: Simulating the described process directly is already fast enough when both the operation count and array size are small.
hints: The operation is fully specified: repeatedly find the leftmost minimum and multiply it - there is no shortcut needed, just do exactly that k times.
Track the current array state and, on each of the k rounds, scan for the first index achieving the minimum value.
Multiply that index's value by multiplier, write it back into the array, and move to the next round; return the array after all k rounds.

edge_inputs.description: all-tied values, tie always broken by first index
duplicate minimums scattered through the array
multiplier=1 is a no-op regardless of k
minimum shifts between two tied positions across rounds
large gap between smallest and largest values

stress.description: random array near max length, several rounds
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 100) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, 10)
    multiplier = rng.randint(1, 5)
    return [nums, k, multiplier]

oracle_python:
import heapq


class Solution:
    def getFinalState(self, nums, k, multiplier):
        nums = nums[:]
        heap = [(v, i) for i, v in enumerate(nums)]
        heapq.heapify(heap)
        for _ in range(k):
            v, i = heapq.heappop(heap)
            nums[i] = v * multiplier
            heapq.heappush(heap, (nums[i], i))
        return nums
